channel.title: Comments for page "Security Clearance Levels"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "Security Clearance Levels"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fair enough. I predict it will be a lot of contestant's 2nd or 3rd choice. Personally, I go with 9999, 9900, 9876, or 9005 (in that order).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not necessarily. Maybe someone will participate in the Series X contest and want the number 9341 when the time to pick slots happens.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, the first three crappy coldposts to go in that slot. After that, it will be taken by some completely unrelated SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: When we reach series 10 we all know what SCP-9341 will be!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Alright, thanks for the info. I'm thinking of making an agent kind of like that

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Depends on the kind of agent. Field agents typically work out in the field, but there's plenty of room for stories about field agents who spend part of their time offsite and part of their time doing administrative duties or investigations in facilities.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have a question,
Agents only work outside of the facility, or do they also work inside the facility?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I hope SCP Unity is able to get the key cards matched up with this canon.

item.title: Admin Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Dr A Pancake, this post is essentially contentless, and given how many comments you've made that are similarly low-content, you're getting spammy here. Please refrain from making similar posts from now on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well this helps

item.title: Re: Ranking Up
item.content:encoded:
As it's described here, the Class system is tied less to Rank / Clearance Levels and more to the personnel's position and role within the Foundation. Class A and B are probably the rarest: given to few high-ranking and critical staff for whom exposure to risks is not acceptable; and when in danger they are considered top-priority for rescue/evacuation. Note that while a Site's Chief of Security is among the top-ranked in a facility and would probably have Level 4 clearance, they would still be Class C because they are expected to engage in dangerous incidents rather than evacuate.

Class C, I think, would apply to the majority of staff. Trusted recruits into the Foundation for whom exposure to risk and anomalies is deemed reasonable in completing their duties. Class D is for those staff recruited by… whichever of the 'lesss voluntary' methods one headcanons they're sourced from, and who can be moreso considered "expendible" (within reason, of course).

So, changes in a particuar individual's Class designation would probably be rare and likely to accompany an inter-departmental transfer (say, from Security Dept. into Administrative Dept.) or a major promotion (e.g: from Sr. Researcher to the Site's Head of Research)

item.title: Re: Ranking Up
item.content:encoded:
How does one rank up from, let's say Class D, to a Class C, and so on?

…they don't? From what I'm reading, class ranks depend on an individual's role within the Foundation as employees/test subjects. Class A are the O5 Councilmembers, Class B are senior researchers and high-level containment specialists, Class C are regular researchers/staff, etc.

Or are the Class ranks are determined by the status of person recruited?

I don't think so, since the Foundation wouldn't recruit people unless they have the skills necessary for the job. A person who is super rich but has no lab skills would not be recruited as a researcher since they'd be useless in a lab setting.

item.title: Ranking Up
item.content:encoded:
How does one rank up from, let's say Class D, to a Class C, and so on?

Or are the Class ranks are determined by the status of person recruited?
For example, Class D are death row inmates.
Maybe Class Cs are Civilians.
Class Bs maybe are Trusted Civilians, or a relative of a Foundation's Scientist.
Class As are influential peoples, rich, and/or individuals with clean criminal records, did a reputable and requirement-meeting service to the country, and currently now is volunteering to do the jobs at the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Should the task forces page be linked to twice in the same sentence? It seems a little redundant…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I noticed that it's been mentioned that Class D personnel are no longer explicitly drawn from death row, however according to another user on the site, even saying that "most" Class D personnel have been convicted of murder is inaccurate.

Since that's now apparently the case, can we change the wording of the Class D entry from this:

Class D personnel are typically drawn worldwide from the ranks of prison inmates convicted of violent crimes, especially those on death row.

To this:

Class D personnel are often drawn worldwide from the ranks of prison inmates convicted of violent crimes, including those on death row.

As it stands, it somewhat unambiguously states the following facts about Class D's:

  • The typical Class D has been convicted of a violent crime.
  • Protocol especially targets those on death row.
  • Most Class D personnel are convicted of a violent crime of which an accepted punishment is execution.

Worldwide, the only "violent crime" that the vast majority of countries will punish with death is murder, therefore it is unambiguously implied that most Class D personnel have been convicted of murder. Yes, I'm aware that some countries will execute people for adultery or sacrilege, but they're in the minority, and neither of those are "violent crimes."

I feel that, if we're all in agreement that "most Class D personnel have been convicted of murder" is a false statement, the guide should be rewritten so that it doesn't imply that they have been.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very nice read! :D So… just to confirm:

There are 13 O5's total, worldwide, nobody knows their actual names for security reasons, and they don't typically come into contact with anyone below director status, nor are any objects regardless of rating allowed near them, aye?

D-Class are basically the cranes and bulldozers, handling the most dangerous of the objects so that the more essential staff don't risk themselves. They are also highly expendable./

Security Officers are basically there to throw 9mm rounds at an SCP and yell into a radio until Response Teams or an MTF rolls in.

Is that about right? I just wanna make sure I understood it all. I have a tendency to be, well, a little mentally inept lmao

item.title: Re: JUST SAYING
item.content:encoded:
It's generally good to mention both security clearance and position when assigning tasks.

Also, incidentally, I think you accidentally a word.

item.title: JUST SAYING
item.content:encoded:
Every time someone assigns a task to personel, and uses thier security clearance instead of position, it makes pee a little blood.

Just sayin.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This has always bothered me about this page, the clearance levels really don't make sense (at least they aren't reflective of the real world use of security clearance systems).

I mean, they just seem to be offered as a quick analog for pay grade, which is very specifically not how they work in the real world. Information is classified based on the amount of damage it's release could do. Each higher level of classification is more stringently protected. Seemingly mundane pieces of information are classified at high levels because they can combine with other seemingly mundane pieces of information to cause severe damage to the classifying organization.

The result of this is you have people on maintenance crews with top secret security clearances. Sure, the battle order for global mobilization of all MTFs in K-scenarios might be classified at the highest level, but the reliability of the back up electrical system at Site 13 & three quarters, and the transport schedule of SCP-260 targets would be too. Any information which is an exploitable vulnerability to the core mission of the Foundation (Secure, Contain, Protect) is going to, by definition, be classified the highest level of secrecy.

So all of this goes back to the fundamental approach to explaining classification guidelines in this article: Classification is about information, not people. Information is classified, your clearance is the highest clearance of any information you may potentially interact with. Operations (the giant, forgotten arm of the Foundation) would be littered with Level 4&5 personnel who make 30-50k/year. IT would be exclusively level 5 people. Clearance level 1 & 2 wouldn't exist (those individuals would require escorts through any classified locations). Almost all of research personnel not working on Keters would be classified at level 3.

There is this overwhelming trend to use clearance as shorthand for rank, when really, clearance is entirely redundant in the Foundation-verse. The veil is the most closely held secret the Foundation has, as it could do as much or more damage than any other (non cognitohazardous) piece of information were to to leak. You reflexively must be cleared to know about the veil to work for the Foundation, which means they trust you to keep every other secret too.

This is even addressed (almost) on the "Writing" tab which makes the poor explanations of 0-5 level even more frustrating to read.

item.title: Re: D-Class Clearance
item.content:encoded:
Erm… no?

Level 1 security clearances are given to personnel working in proximity to but with no direct, indirect, or informational access to anomalous objects or entities in containment. Level 1 security clearances are typically granted to personnel working in clerical, logistics, or janitorial positions at facilities with containment capability or otherwise must handle sensitive information.

item.title: D-Class Clearance
item.content:encoded: Does this mean that D-Class have Level 1 clearance, since they are testing SCPs on a need to know basis?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is there a tale about that? Would you mind if I attempted that Ael?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
An even more outlandish idea is that the O5s don't even know they're O5s. I once theorized that the 13 O5s in fact don't even know that the SCP Foundation exists; they do not have fancy bunkers or constant bodyguards, but are actually a bunch of regular people who think they're in some kind of fancy immersive roleplay in which they are given status reports, make decisions, and are 'scored' on the outcome of each 'scenario'.

That HAS to be a tale.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a good question, and one whose answers are subject to a lot of personal interpretation.

To speak for myself:

  • I personally think that O5s are in fact not promoted from within. They have to be dispassionate observers, and it's hard to do that when you've been on the front line. Since O5s are not allowed to be exposed to skips, it just makes sense to me that low-level researchers with direct contact cannot ever be O5s.
    • An even more outlandish idea is that the O5s don't even know they're O5s. I once theorized that the 13 O5s in fact don't even know that the SCP Foundation exists; they do not have fancy bunkers or constant bodyguards, but are actually a bunch of regular people who think they're in some kind of fancy immersive roleplay in which they are given status reports, make decisions, and are 'scored' on the outcome of each 'scenario'.
  • I think that the Foundation has a decently sophisticated process for detecting mind-affecting effect symptoms, as evidenced by things like "Does the Black Moon howl?". It's imperfect, but that's also why researchers who are exposed to anomalies have to do things like go through mandatory quarantine and regular psychiatric evaluation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What safeguards are in place to ensure someone negatively influenced by an anomalous object early in his or her career does not carry that corruption with them to the position of O-5?
It seems like it would behoove some of the smarter sentient cognitohazards to "get in on the ground floor" so to speak by implanting class-C personnel with post-hypnotic suggestions (or what have you) that remain dormant until that person has risen to a rank of sufficient authority within the foundation.
The safeguards might not necessarily be a "foolproof" method of screening O-5 candidates, but rather a system of checks and balances that assume at least one O-5 is compromized at any given time and act to mitigate the potential for mischief.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great job guys. I'm glad there is a clarification about the mandatory termination of the D-Class now completely optional. And that it's official that there is 13 O5s.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: On the flip side, it gives the central background more of a feel of a system that had logic and experience put into it, rather than a list of disjointed terms that make no sense taken in isolation. To each their own?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Count me in as saying I'm absolutely not a fan of Class-A through Class-C; they're not used anywhere other than this article, and if they're only there for the purposes of answering a question that few people were asking anyway, then it's just one additional layer of jargon for newbie writers to misuse.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So much new reading material for this week.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this is well done and logical. I've been a member of this wiki for over two years, and I have wondered at times what happened to Class A-C personnel. One thing that may help in these classifications are other examples. Obviously an O5 is Class A, but so may the leaders of powerful nations (which is not necessarily the President, Premier, etc). Just my two cents after the fact. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you have criticisms and can be bothered to respond at all, then courtesy dictates that you should say them. To cop out like this and imply that there are many who would disagree with what's been written is both non-constructive and rude towards the people who spent a lot of time working on the guide.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You did a spectacular job here, Aelanna.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't have the time or space to discuss this, I'll let the people with same objections, which there are not few, speak for themselves.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you absolutely can't bring yourself to work in the paradigm outlined here, it's not a complicated matter to write around the mention of security clearance levels.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is supremely useful - I was just writing up an article draft and wanted to check staff titles, only to find a whole new comprehensive guide on the matter! My thanks to all involved in reworking this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
However, according to a lot of articles, it isn't right.

If you have specific articles that contradict the new guide information, please bring them to my attention. This guide was created using a combination of old guide information, common site usage, and real-world security clearance procedure.

[…] I can't think of any reason to have class A-C, unless it's being appended to the number clearance […]

A-C exist because newbies often ask what A-C are. This sliding scale also allowed us to fold E into the same scale, and thus streamline the entire concept of A-E. It also adds a further undertone of competence to the Foundation by implying that the Foundation has very sophisticated disaster response and recovery, something that is far more plausible than the royal clusterfuck that every containment breach seems to sound like. It's not intended to be appended to number clearances, nor does any of this require that articles be changed.

There's no longer any room to breathe.

Of all the guides that were overhauled, this was the one with the least room for interpretation, the only major salient points being Class D personnel and titles. If there is any particular point that sticks out to you and makes you feel that it restricts your ability to write, then by all means, please point them out. The general intent of all of the guide overhauls was to create a medium that was more inspiring rather than restrictive; no one I have spoken to in the process of writing this felt that it unduly restricted anyone's ability to portray the Foundation within their personal headcanon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is pretty cool. Don't think I'll use them, but a damn nice piece of work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good and simple.

Good, simple, implausible, and incorrectly used. This overhaul came to be because common usage and staff interpretation disagreed with the actual words that were on it.

I am saying this because many of the restrictions about A-C classes overlap or could be overlapped with the current clearances (O5s with As, Site directors with Bs) and so on with little effort.

That's not the point. The point was to explain why we have a Class D when there's no A-C, something that actually comes up quite often when people ask you about Foundation backstory.

Also, I feel that D-classes somehow got the suspense and horror around them cut out with no real reason.

The reason is because many people felt that this was implausibly and over-the-top ridiculous. It shattered peoples' suspension of disbelief because there simply aren't that many death row convicts around.

A horrible thing that instills fear that the next civilian made into Ds can be literally YOU.

Same with the monthly executions.

Many people would call this forced and contrived, rather than actual existential horror. Among those people also happen to be the majority of the administration.

If this is a progress from the old "scary and simple" Foundation to the modern bureucratic Foundation […] I don't think the steps mentioned above were good steps to take.

As a reminder to everyone, these guides were overhauled with the direct input and blessing of most of the administration and staff. This represents what we have wanted the guides to be for a very long time — well over a year, in fact — and have been thoroughly edited over the course of literally months to address every minor detail.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: First of all, I'd like to say that this is an extremely impressive document. However, according to a lot of articles, it isn't right. Several standard definitions have been changed, the D-class program has lost quite a bit of punch, and I can't think of any reason to have class A-C, unless it's being appended to the number clearance in which case it's a little late for that. Frankly, seeing it all laid out like this so clear and concise(actually it's much longer), it's like a corset. There's no longer any room to breathe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I saw this when it was still a draft on the Nala wiki, and I'm so glad that it's made its way to the main site now. Great job guys!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, this feels far clearer than the previous version! I'm glad to know what terminology we should use for non-research SCP personnel, and the A, B and C classes feel perfectly coherent with E and D. Thanks!

As a side note, I feel fortunate that I was here to witness this shift. It comes to show the continuous refinement in contents and background, and makes me feel that this community is both alive and writing an elaborate world beneath its tales and articles, changing so that it remains stable, and complex, and engaging. It is nice. : )

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As for the A-E personnel, I always thought the old system was very economical in it's designations: Class D with explicit information who they are and what do they do as the only class to be mentioned, and Class E as the seldom person that came into contact with an uncontained SCPs. Good and simple.
To me, the A-C classes seem excessive and unneeded, only cluttering the already known information about Foundation employees. I am saying this because many of the restrictions about A-C classes overlap or could be overlapped with the current clearances (O5s with As, Site directors with Bs) and so on with little effort. What I see here is unneccessary cluttering of the already established canon, making something that can be simply defined, for example, by rank or headcanon, by too much information (Level 4 B Class Site Director Commander Shepard. Urgh.)

Also, I feel that D-classes somehow got the suspense and horror around them cut out with no real reason. A good example is Protocol 12: A horrible thing that instills fear that the next civilian made into Ds can be literally YOU. Now, it's just another kind of prisoner, convict, a person in a refugee camp, hm. The "Other civilian sources" do the old concept little justice, because they hint that there are sources for expendable civilians (say, Sri Lanka slums) and the first time reader would not get as much goosebumps as during the original execution.
Same with the monthly executions. It was some major fleshing out of the Foundation, what does it do in pursuing its goals, and if authors' headcanon said otherwise, the author went and proposed his own interpretation that the executions don't take place without hinting from any other source.
If this is a progress from the old "scary and simple" Foundation to the modern bureucratic Foundation I don't know, but even though the majority of the work on this page and its' following pages is well done, I don't think the steps mentioned above were good steps to take.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A number of things I love about this revamp:

  • It makes sense. It doesn't introduce a ton of logic holes, unlike the last system.
  • Level 0 through 5 now actually make sense. (As they should, given that Mann actually works with clearance levels, and Aelanna has done so as well!)
  • Level 5, "Thaumiel", is a really fun addition to fluff. (I think that was Mann/Aelanna.)
  • Class A through C are fun, interesting additions, the kind of stuff that the last system was trying to use, except it merged them confusingly with clearance level and was too light on detail to actually be interesting.
  • Class D and Class E are excellent revamps of old concepts.
  • Class D (probably the most important part of the document) is completely compatible with current canon yet it no longer horribly breaks suspension of disbelief! This was a tough nut to crack, and I think what we came up with is excellent.
  • Staff Titles are now useful and make sense! Yay!
  • The Writing Guide is a really nice behind-the-curtain essay. I imagine it will be helpful to many.
  • Overall, this page is now actually extremely helpful to writers wanting to actually write shit about the Foundation!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To address this one point, at least, doctor is both a title and a profession and it's often misused. Check the discussion thread there for the long-form explanation from Hornby.

To paraphrase here, though, "Dr." is a title you're allowed to use if you hold a terminal degree in your field. However, it's not your professional title unless you are in fact a medical doctor, and it's certainly not what be on the nameplate on your office or your ID/business card. This list addresses professional titles, not titles earned from academia.

The correct full title, by the way, would be "Dr. Bailey, Senior Researcher, Extradimensional Research Department" in this case.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've always been under the impression that "Doctor" was a personal title, rather than an organizational title. Having it here would be the equivalent of putting "PhD" or "MD" in the guide, which is obviously unnecessary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would like to thank staff for overhauling this guide… but I can't, because they've taken a half-page guide that didn't need to be overhauled in the first place and put it up to an entire page, two tabs long, and have tried to set precedents that I don't think a lot of people will be very happy with.

Firstly, credit where its due: The staff titles section was a good idea, and is executed well, for the most part (even if we still don't know what HMCL means). If it means that people will have a little more diversity when it comes to calling agents "agents". However, I highly dislike the fact that it omits "doctor" and replaces it with "researcher", when the two should be interchangeable; also which takes a less amount of time to write, "Multi-Universal Researcher Bailey" or "Dr. Bailey"?. Secondly, the security clearance expansions themselves, at the top, are also a good idea (the fact that Thaumiel is now a part of site canon makes my heart jump with joy a little). Finally, the bit in the writing guide with the D-Class mentioning they might not be killed is a good idea, even if 99% of the articles featuring them say or imply that there's a monthly termination in some way.

What I don't like is the Class A though C personnel, or Class-E personnel. There is no precedent for the phrase Class-A, Class-B, or Class-C personnel. If people read this guide and see that there's literally not a single thing on the site that mentions them, what are they going to think? I would think "Why would they have it in the guide at all?" I'm not going to use them, that's for sure; if you wanted to establish canon like this and set a precedent, you should have done this when the site was first starting off and not now, almost 6 or 7 years on.

There's also barely any precedent for Class-E, other than the fact that it was in the original guide; exactly one SCP that I can find, SCP-1337, uses Class-E personnel. What there is precedent for, however, is Class-E anomalies. From what I can tell, this came from the original Visual Records pages on the wiki had the term "E-###" in the captions, and the term is used by some writers (including myself) to describe SCPs that haven't received classification and/or reports as of yet, and also for lower-scale Anomalous Items in some stories (not the Log of Anomalous Items, but still.) There's more precedent for E-Class anomalies than there is for E-Class personnel at this point, and they don't even get a nod in any guide at all.

Sadly, the things I dislike in this outweigh the good, so allow me to show my dissatisfaction with a downvote.

item.title: 2013 Guide Overhaul
item.content:encoded:
This guide has been rewritten as part of the 2014 background guide overhaul. The original version of this page can be found in archive form here.

Changes From Original Version

  • Security Clearance Levels have been cleaned up and made more consistent with real-world security clearance systems, along with notes on "need to know" and how that works.
  • Class A-C have been defined, Class D has been cleaned up, and Class E has been reworked to maintain the original sentiment while making it more in line with A-D.
  • Class D personnel are no longer explicitly terminated at the end of the month; the new definition has more room for headcanon.
  • Class D personnel are no longer explicitly drawn from death row convict populations.
  • Added Employee Title short list to combat the perception that the Foundation is made up entirely of only "Agents", "Researchers", and "O5s".

Credits

This guide was written with the help of:

EchoFourDelta — SCP senior staff, former United States Marine, special operations infantryman, marksmanship/close-quarters combat instructor, and armory technician.
DrEverettMann — SCP administrator, active duty United States Air Force electronic warfare/intelligence specialist.
thedeadlymoose — SCP administrator.
TroyL — SCP administrator.
Aelanna — SCP veteran author, software engineer formerly employed by a United States federal contractor.
